blurb: A tough Southwest native shrub that flowers white roses through the warm months and follows them with showy feathery pink seedhead plumes — drought-proof, deer-resistant, and built for hot, lean ground.
seasons: White rose-like flowers late spring–fall, then feathery pink seedhead plumes that persist; semi-evergreen fine foliage
wildlife: Native bees and butterflies; seeds and cover for birds

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 4 and reliable through Boulder's zone 5b–6a, taking high-altitude sun, drying wind, and cold without trouble.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant container stock in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, giving roots time to establish before hard winter; site it in a hot, lean, sharply drained spot. In pots its roots are far less cold-hardy, so sink the container or move it to an unheated shelter over winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part sun; it flowers and keeps its dense form best in all-day Front Range sun.[1][2]
care.soil: Thrives in lean, gritty, well-drained ground — clay, loam, sand, or shallow rocky soils — and is at home in Boulder's alkaline clay; the one requirement is sharp drainage.[1][2]
care.water: Very drought tolerant once established; water occasionally the first season, then little to no supplemental water in our semi-arid climate.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 4–6 ft apart to match its mature spread and let air move through the open canopy.[1][2]
care.propagation: Grows readily from fresh untreated seed that hasn't been allowed to dry; dried seed benefits from about 30 days of cold-moist stratification near 41°F. It also propagates from stem cuttings and by separating suckers from the spreading roots.[2][3]
care.bloom: White, rose-like flowers open from late spring through summer into fall (roughly May–September), followed by feathery pink seed-head plumes that hold into winter for a long second act.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Very low care; avoid shearing — prune selectively, and if it gets rangy cut it back hard to a low mound in spring to rejuvenate. It can self-sow, so pull unwanted seedlings.[1][2]
care.troubles: Largely pest- and disease-free in dry, well-drained sites; the main issue is rot or flopping in heavy, wet, or over-irrigated soil.[1][2]

references.name: NC State Extension Plant Toolbox — Fallugia paradoxa
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Fallugia paradoxa

edible.summary: Not a food plant — grown for its flowers and feathery seed plumes, with no documented edible use.[3]
edible.caution: Apache plume has no known culinary use; it's valued as ornamental and wildlife browse, not as human food. No part is notably poisonous, but none should be eaten.[3]
